prevalence.source: Allergy/anaphylaxis epidemiology syntheses; WHO allergy context
prevalence.trend: increasing recognition in emergency care (food, drug and sting triggers)

burden.dalyPerCapita: low; preventable deaths with timely epinephrine
burden.mortalityRate: Low but higher where epinephrine access is limited

vulnerablePopulations: rural areas with delayed emergency access
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical criteria (acute onset with airway/breathing/circulation compromise or hypotension after exposure); tryptase supports diagnosis when available

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; event-driven diagnosis and education
publicHealthSystem.estimatedPatients: Millions with lifetime risk; acute events far fewer
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Variable; depends on epinephrine access and follow-up allergy care

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: ED-based diagnosis; allergy referral for trigger identification and prevention planning
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Immediate IM epinephrine, airway/oxygen/IV fluids as needed, observation for biphasic reactions; action plan and availability of auto-injectors where feasible
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Limited auto-injector availability/cost, under-recognition in primary care, uneven access to allergists
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Standard emergency protocols effective when implemented; growing urban allergy services
